title: Tuples & Sets
subtitle: Two new ways to hold a bunch of things: one that never changes, and one that hates duplicates.
goal: Learn what tuples and sets are, when to use each, how to unpack tuples, and how to combine sets with union, intersection, and difference.

blocks.heading: Remember lists? Meet their cousins 👋
blocks.html:
You already know lists — boxes that hold lots of things in order, like ["apple", "banana"].

Today you meet two new kinds of boxes:

  • 📦 A tuple — like a list, but locked shut. Once you make it, you can't change it.
  • 🎒 A set — a box that refuses duplicates. Put the same thing in twice and it only keeps one.

Let's open them up!

blocks.heading: Tuples: lists that never change 🔒
blocks.html:
A tuple is made with round brackets ( ) instead of square ones. You read items the same way you read a list — with [0], [1], and so on.

The big difference: once a tuple is made, it can never be changed. That makes tuples perfect for things that should stay fixed — like a point on a map.

blocks.caption: A GPS coordinate that never changes
blocks.code:
point = (12, 5)
print("X is", point[0])
print("Y is", point[1])
print("The full point is", point)
blocks.explain: We made a tuple with ( ). We read items with [0] and [1], just like a list. A tuple is great here because a saved location shouldn't accidentally change.

blocks.html: Why lock it? Use a tuple when the group of things belongs together and should NOT change — like a date (year, month, day) or a screen size (width, height).

blocks.heading: Unpacking: pull a tuple apart ✨
blocks.html:
Here's a neat trick. You can pull the pieces of a tuple into their own names in one line. This is called unpacking.

Instead of writing point[0] and point[1], you just say x, y = point. Now x is the first value and y is the second. So tidy!

blocks.caption: Unpacking a point into x and y
blocks.code:
point = (12, 5)
x, y = point
print("x =", x)
print("y =", y)
blocks.explain: x, y = point gives the first value to x and the second to y in a single line. This is also how Python lets a function hand back two answers at once.

blocks.question: What makes a tuple different from a list?
options: A tuple can hold only numbers
A tuple cannot be changed once it is made
A tuple can only hold one item
A tuple prints in a different color
blocks.explain: A tuple is immutable — once you create it, its contents are locked. Lists, on the other hand, can be changed.

blocks.heading: Sets: no duplicates allowed 🚫🚫
blocks.html:
A set is a box that automatically throws away repeats. You make one with curly brackets { } (or with set()).

Put the same thing in three times? The set keeps just one copy. That makes sets perfect for answering "how many DIFFERENT things are here?"

blocks.caption: Counting unique visitors
blocks.code:
visitors = {"Sam", "Mia", "Sam", "Leo", "Mia"}
print("The set:", visitors)
print("How many different people:", len(visitors))

visitors.add("Zoe")
print("After Zoe arrives:", len(visitors))
blocks.explain: Even though Sam and Mia were typed twice, the set keeps just one of each. add() puts a new item in. len() counts how many UNIQUE items there are.

blocks.html: Sets are also super fast at answering "is this thing inside?" Asking "Mia" in visitors gives True in a snap, even with millions of items.
Heads up: a set does NOT keep things in order, and you can't grab item[0] from it. If order matters, use a list or tuple instead.

blocks.heading: Mixing sets: union, intersection, difference 🤝
blocks.html:
Sets have three magic powers for comparing two groups:

  • Union with | — everyone from BOTH groups (no repeats).
  • Intersection with & — only the things in BOTH groups (their common stuff).
  • Difference with - — things in the first group but NOT the second.

blocks.caption: Finding common friends
blocks.explain: | joins both groups together, & keeps just the friends they share, and - keeps the friends only Ana has. Great for comparing two groups fast!

blocks.heading: List vs Tuple vs Set — the cheat sheet 📋
blocks.html:
KindMade withCan change?Allows duplicates?Keeps order?
List[ ]✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes
Tuple( )❌ No (locked)✅ Yes✅ Yes
Set{ }✅ Yes❌ No (unique only)❌ No

Pick a list for an ordered group you'll edit, a tuple for a fixed group, and a set when you only want the different items.

blocks.question: What does {"a", "b", "a", "b"} become?
options: A set with 2 items: a and b
blocks.explain: Sets throw away duplicates, so the four entries collapse into just two unique ones: a and b.

blocks.question: Which symbol finds the items shared by BOTH sets?
blocks.explain: The & symbol gives the intersection — only the items found in both sets, like common friends.

blocks.title: Unpack a coordinate
blocks.instructions: A tuple named spot holds (8, 3). Use tuple unpacking to put the two values into x and y, then print them. Finish the line that starts with x, y =.
blocks.starter:
spot = (8, 3)
x, y = ___
print("x =", x)
print("y =", y)
blocks.solution:
spot = (8, 3)
x, y = spot
print("x =", x)
print("y =", y)
blocks.passMsg: Perfect unpacking — x and y popped right out of the tuple!
blocks.failMsg: Set x, y = spot so the two values land in x and y.

blocks.title: Count the unique fruits
blocks.instructions: Turn the list of fruits into a set to remove duplicates, then print how many DIFFERENT fruits there are. Fill in the blank with set(fruits).
blocks.solution:
fruits = ["apple", "pear", "apple", "plum", "pear"]
unique = set(fruits)
print("Different fruits:", len(unique))
blocks.passMsg: Nice — the set squished the repeats and counted just the unique ones!
blocks.failMsg: Use set(fruits) to drop the duplicates, then len() to count what's left.

challenge.title: Class clubs combiner
challenge.instructions: Two clubs have member sets. Print the union (everyone), the intersection (kids in BOTH clubs), and the difference (kids only in the art club). Use |, &, and - on the two sets below.
challenge.hint: Use art_club | music_club for everyone, art_club & music_club for the shared kids, and art_club - music_club for the art-only kids.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Club master! You combined sets like a pro — Day 11 complete. Tuples lock, sets de-dupe, and you've got both!
